{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 2","old":"DEPTH = 1","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 8","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 32","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"class CIFAR10Net(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        super().__init__()
        channels = BASE_CHANNELS
        bias = not USE_BATCHNORM
        stem_layers: list[nn.Module] = [nn.Conv2d(INPUT_CHANNELS, channels, 3, padding=1, bias=bias)]
        if USE_BATCHNORM:
            stem_layers.append(nn.BatchNorm2d(channels))
        stem_layers.append(nn.ReLU(inplace=True))
        self.stem = nn.Sequential(*stem_layers)

        blocks: list[nn.Module] = [
            nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1, bias=bias),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(channels) if USE_BATCHNORM else nn.Identity(),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
        ]
        for _ in range(DEPTH):
            blocks.append(ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM))
        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(*blocks)
        self.pool = nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d((1, 1))
        self.classifier = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Flatten(),
            nn.Linear(channels, FC_HIDDEN),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            nn.Dropout(DROPOUT_RATE),
            nn.Linear(FC_HIDDEN, NUM_CLASSES),
        )

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        x = self.stem(x)
        x = self.blocks(x)
        x = self.pool(x)
        return self.classifier(x)","old":"class CIFAR10Net(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        super().__init__()
        channels = BASE_CHANNELS
        bias = not USE_BATCHNORM
        stem_layers: list[nn.Module] = [nn.Conv2d(INPUT_CHANNELS, channels, 3, padding=1, bias=bias)]
        if USE_BATCHNORM:
            stem_layers.append(nn.BatchNorm2d(channels))
        stem_layers.append(nn.ReLU(inplace=True))
        self.stem = nn.Sequential(*stem_layers)
        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1, bias=bias),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(channels) if USE_BATCHNORM else nn.Identity(),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
        )
        self.pool = nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d((1, 1))
        self.classifier = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Flatten(),
            nn.Linear(channels, FC_HIDDEN),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            nn.Dropout(DROPOUT_RATE),
            nn.Linear(FC_HIDDEN, NUM_CLASSES),
        )

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        x = self.stem(x)
        x = self.blocks(x)
        x = self.pool(x)
        return self.classifier(x)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The baseline network is severely underpowered for 256 CIFAR-10 steps. A modest width increase plus one extra residual block is a simple capacity upgrade that should improve early-learning quality without adding optimizer or loop complexity.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Under a fixed 256-step budget, increasing batch size lets the model see substantially more training examples. The current run appears time-safe, so trading some per-step cost for much higher data throughput is a strong next move.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest gain so far came from increasing data throughput under the fixed-step budget. Pushing batch size further is the simplest way to expose the model to many more examples within the same 256 updates.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The model now processes far more examples per update than the earlier successful runs, but the learning rate has not been scaled to match. A straightforward LR increase is the cleanest next step to better exploit the larger fixed-step batch regime.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The current setup is training aggressively on large batches and high learning rate. Label smoothing is a very cheap regularization change that often improves validation loss for CIFAR-10 classifiers without changing the training loop or model shape.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The current recipe is already strong on batch size, LR, schedule, and mild smoothing. A clean optimizer-family swap to AdamW is a low-complexity way to test whether decoupled weight decay gives better validation loss in this high-throughput regime.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Optimization settings now look reasonably tuned, and recent gains from optimizer and regularization tweaks have become small. A moderate width increase is the simplest capacity test to see whether the stronger training recipe can profit from a slightly larger feature backbone within the same fixed-step budget.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The widened backbone produced the largest gain so far, suggesting the current regime is still capacity-limited. Adding one more residual block is the next clean architecture test and should increase representational depth with less cost than another big width jump.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 2","old":"DEPTH = 3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The added residual block made validation loss worse and substantially increased runtime. Reverting that depth increase keeps the stronger width change while removing the extra complexity that did not pay off.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 128","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The widened convolutional trunk is now much stronger than the original head. Expanding the classifier MLP is a low-cost capacity increase that may let the larger backbone translate its features into lower validation loss without materially affecting runtime.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 256","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The previous head expansion improved validation loss, suggesting the classifier is still a mild bottleneck. Doubling the hidden size again is a cheap capacity increase compared with widening the convolutional trunk further, so it is a good next layout test.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 512","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The classifier head has produced two consecutive meaningful gains, while convolutional depth increases did not. Expanding the hidden layer again is the most direct follow-up to test whether the bottleneck remains in the low-cost MLP head rather than the more expensive trunk.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.1","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The model now has a much larger classifier head and still uses no dropout. A small dropout rate is a cheap regularization test that may improve validation loss without materially changing the overall optimization recipe.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.1","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Dropout hurt badly in this short-budget setting, so the simplest and most defensible move is to remove it and restore the stronger no-dropout regime before trying other ideas.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 1024","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The classifier head has been a consistent source of gains, and increasing it is still much cheaper than making the convolutional trunk larger. One more head expansion is the cleanest remaining capacity test given the observed trajectory.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 2048","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 1024","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The head-size sweep has been consistently productive and remains much cheaper than enlarging the convolutional body. A final larger hidden layer is the most plausible remaining capacity upgrade given the observed trend.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 4096","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 2048","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Every successful late-stage improvement has come from enlarging the classifier head, with only modest runtime impact. {"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 4096","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 8192","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The jump from 4096 to 8192 hurt validation loss, so the final move should restore the strongest known head size rather than spend the last step on a lower-confidence new direction.","secondary_modes":[]}
